Final Step: Narrowing Down To Just Five Companies

In this step, we construct three separate lists of five stocks each, with different sets of goals, dividend income, and risk levels.

The lists are:

1) Relatively Safe (Low-yield) Dividend list,

2) Moderately High Dividend List,

3) Ultra High Dividend List, and

4) A combined list of the above three (duplicates removed).

Out of the top 50 (or 49 this time), we make our judgment calls to make these three lists, so basically, the selections are based on our research and perceptions. So, while most of the filtering until now was based on automated criteria, the last step is pretty much subjective. We try to make each of the three lists highly diversified among various sectors and industry segments and try to ensure that the safety of dividends matches the overall risk profile of the group. We certainly encourage readers to do further research on the highlighted names.

Nonetheless, here are our three final lists for this month:

Final A-List (Relatively Safe Income):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 2.75% and 4.25%.
  • At least three or four names with 'A' rated.
  • Solid track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 4.16%

  • (V)
  • (UNH)
  • (MS)
  • (CVX)
  • (LYB)

Table-1A: A-LIST (Conservative Income)

We think this set of five companies (in the A-List) would form a solid diversified group of dividend companies that would be appealing to income-seeking and conservative investors, including retirees and near-retirees. The average yield is 4.16%, which is over 3x of what the S&P 500 pays. The average dividend history is nearly 19 years. Also, four out of five companies have an excellent rating of 'A-' or higher, while the fifth one has an investment-grade rating. If you must need higher dividends, consider the B-List or the C-List, presented later.

As usual, we will provide a brief synopsis of each of the companies selected here; however, we will not go into too many details, as that will make the article unduly long.

V (Visa):

Just like last month, we are including Visa in our A-list to provide a boost to dividend growth and stability for our conservative group of investors. The dividend is a lowly 0.65%; however, what we ensure is the average yield for the group (of five stocks) stays reasonable. Visa and Mastercard (MA) are two businesses that operate like a duopoly in the payment processing industry, almost globally. There are other players, but they have a long way to go to catch up with these two titans. We like Visa slightly better than Mastercard as Visa trades comparatively cheaper than Mastercard. The 5-year and 10-year dividend growth rates are excellent at 15.1% and 17.6%, respectively, and we believe it will be a good addition to the A-List to balance out the yield and growth. Besides, for the A-List, we try to ensure that at least four out of five companies have a credit rating of A- or better.

MS (Morgan Stanley):

Morgan Stanley has been scoring well in our ratings since last year. Since then, the price has moved up significantly, and it is not as attractive (in terms of valuation) as it was in 2024. So, shares are not cheap. Even though it has no direct impact from tariff-related matters, its success still depends on the growth of the broader economy.

With all that said, we see many positives with the company. It is one of the largest investment banks when you count its revenue. It has roughly $5 trillion in clients' assets, with a majority of it under the supervision of their financial advisors. The past 5-year and 10-year dividend growth has been exceptionally good at nearly 22% and 25%, respectively, and the payout ratio is still reasonable at 44%. However, we will admit that we are unlikely to see that kind of dividend growth in the future, but we can still expect 8% to 12% growth going forward. Last year's dividend increase was nearly 9% in Jul. 2024. However, with a starting yield of roughly 3% and hopefully a steady increase of 8% to 10%, it is still attractive for a large and stable company.

CVX (CHEVRON):

The global oil prices have been trending down for the last few months, and we can pick up some bargains in the industry. If we want safety, an excellent credit rating, stable business, and a reasonably high dividend yield for a large company, Chevron probably tops the list in the energy industry.

Similar to last month, we are including CVX in the A-list for the same reasons as last month. CVX currently offers a highly attractive dividend yield of 5%. Besides, it is a low-cost energy play for the long term.

During the past couple of years, the price of oil has hovered between $70 to $80 a barrel, which was a far cry from peak prices in 2022. However, recently, prices have come down even further to the $60 range. Nonetheless, Chevron is a low-cost producer with its breakeven price in the low to mid-40s. Reflecting the oil prices, the share price is currently 19% below its recent peak and 52-week high. The payout ratio is in the low 60s on an EPS basis and only in the 40s on a cash flow basis. The five-year dividend growth has been a bit tepid but reasonable at 6.5% for an oil major. However, a starting yield of 5% with a 6%+ annual growth would become an 8% to 9% yield in 10 years on a cost basis. The company maintains an excellent credit rating of 'AA' from S&P, which shows its financial strength. Sure, if the economy enters into a recession, the oil prices will fall further. However, we cannot predict future events, and we need to play a long-term game, considering the current lower price to be a buying opportunity.

LYB (LyondellBasell Industries, N.V.):

The materials sector is usually underrepresented in the S&P 500, with only about 2% to 3% weighting, as there are very few large companies in the sector. LyondellBasell is one of the prominent companies in the materials sector and appears to be undervalued. It has been trading at attractive levels for some time, but the prices have undergone further correction due to China-related trade and tariff tensions and the expectation of a global economic slowdown. The current yield is very tempting at nearly 9.5%. All that said, there are some risks with the company, and only patient investors should consider them. This is the only company on the A-list with a credit rating of 'BBB' (all other four have 'A-' or better).

Similar to the previous month, we are including LYB in all our lists (A-list, B-list, and C-list) because of the high yield and undervaluation. LYB deals in petrochemicals and commodity chemicals and has a large manufacturing presence in North America, which provides it access to relatively cheaper natural-gas-based feedstocks. LyondellBasell is headquartered in the Netherlands. The petrochemicals sector has been facing some cyclical headwinds in general, which have impacted the sector's valuations. Morningstar currently assigns a five-star valuation rating to LYB. Morningstar’s own fair-value estimates for LYB have come down by roughly 15%; even then, the current market price is roughly 45% below their estimates.

Final B-List (High Yield, Moderately Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • Yield for the group between 4.75% to 6.25%
  • At least two names with 'A-' (or above) rated.
  • Above-average track record of dividends and growth.

Average yield: 5.70%

  • (V)
  • (MS)
  • (MPLX)
  • (LYB)
  • (RIO).

Note 1: Very often, we include a few low-risk stocks in the B-list and C-list. Also, oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. This is done on purpose. We try to make each of our lists fairly diversified among different sectors/industry segments of the economy. We try to include a few of the highly conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1B: B-LIST (High Yield)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

In the B-List, the overall risk profile of the group becomes slightly elevated compared to the A-List. That said, the group (as a whole) will likely provide safe dividends for many years. This list offers an average yield for the group of 5.70%, an average of 17-plus years of dividend history.

MPLX (MPLX LP):

For the B-List and C-List, we are replacing Chevron with MPLX for the energy sector, mainly to elevate the yield. Please note that the company has a Partnership structure and issues a K-1 tax form (partnership income) instead of the usual 1099 in case of regular dividends.

We have covered MPLX multiple times in our previous articles in this monthly series. Hence, we will skip the details here. MPLX regularly features on our B-list and C-list due to its stable dividend growth and high yield.

RIO (Rio Tinto):

As such, it appears that we have two companies in the B-List (and C-List) that are from the Material sector, the other being LyondellBasell (LYB). However, they operate in totally different industry segments, with LYB being in petrochemicals and RIO operating in mining.

We are including RIO this month in both our B-list and C-list, as it is trading at attractive levels and yields.

Rio Tinto is one of the major mining companies in the world. They also happen to be the world's largest miners in terms of mining operations in iron ore, copper, aluminum, and other minerals. Since the company is in the Commodity business, the demand for its products is tied to the global economic environment. If the global economy sours and slows significantly, the demand for commodities will fall. Another risk to the company is the China factor. China, being the world's factory, accounts for nearly 60% of RIO's sales.

On the positive, a distinct advantage with RIO is that most of its mines are located in safe geographic locations or countries, such as Australia and North America. Secondly, the company has a large portfolio of mines with low operating costs and the ability to stay profitable during the weak commodity cycles. The company also boasts a strong balance sheet and regularly returns a lot of cash to its shareholders.

For dividend investors, the current price is attractive and supports a yield of 7.5%. However, the dividend payout is of a variable nature and is paid on a semi-annual basis (instead of quarterly). The current share price is nearly 20% below the fair value assigned by Morningstar and 17% below the 52-week-high.

Final C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Less Safe):

Criteria for this group:

  • The average yield for the group is between 7.0% to 8.0%
  • All names with investment-grade ratings.
  • Some potential for growth.

Average yield: 8.13%

  • (MPLX)
  • (AES)
  • (LYB)
  • (RIO)
  • (ARCC)

Notes:

Note 1: Oftentimes, a stock can appear in multiple lists. We try to include one or two conservative names in the high-yield list to make the overall group much safer.

Table-1C: C-LIST (Yield-Hungry, Elevated Risk)

NOTE: MPLX is structured as a partnership (not a corporation) and issues a K-1 tax form instead of the usual 1099-Div. Please use due diligence.

AES (AES Corp.)

AES is a utility company that has lost nearly 50% of its share price in the last few months. Most of the losses for AES have been triggered by the expected loss of renewable energy credits/subsidies in the incoming Trump administration. It is correct that AES has invested heavily in the renewables sector, but it still remains a diversified company. It is a typical case of two things being true at the same time. The company has significant revenue and profits from regulated utilities, gas power generation, and generation capacity located outside the USA, including Argentina, Chile, and Colombia, all of which have no impact from the tax subsidies. The international exposure of AES is both a positive and a negative aspect of it. Due to its international exposure, it is considered a more risky utility company than others. On the flip side, it adds to the diversification and reduces one country-specific policy risk. That said, nearly 40% of its revenues and earnings come from the U.S. However, the company is already planning to adjust to the new realities and is making adjustments to its investment plans. The nearly 50% cut in the share price since Oct. 2024 has taken the yield to very attractive levels of nearly 7% for a utility. With the current valuations, the downside is limited. We are adding it to the C-List for its high dividend yield and discounted price.

ARCC (Ares Capital):

Ares Capital is one of the largest BDCs (Business Development Companies) in terms of market capitalization and net asset value. The company invests in small and medium-sized companies and has a diversified portfolio of investments in over 500 companies. Besides, ARCC has a proven record of performing reasonably well both in high and low-interest rate environments.

It generates a solid amount of NII (net Investment income), which currently exceeds well above the $1.2 billion mark on an annual basis. ARCC currently offers an attractive yield of roughly 8.7%.

The company announced its first quarter report at the end of April '25. The report was mixed, with interest income coming below consensus estimates. The interest income from investments came in at $526M (vs. $551M estimates) from $542M in Q4 but still increased from $513M in Q1 2024. The NII was at $365M, beating the consensus of $360M, declined from $371M in the previous quarter, but increased from $325M a year ago.

The core EPS was down at $0.50 a share, down from $0.55 in Q4 2024 and $0.59 in last year's Q1. Based on core EPS, its payout ratio remains roughly at 85% for the last 12 months, though it is creeping up.

=====

Apparently, this list (C-List) is for yield-hungry DGI investors. The yield goes up as much as 8.1%. However, this list is not for conservative investors. As you can see, the average credit rating of this set of companies is much lower than the A-List or even B-List. Dividends are moderately safe in this list, but the yields are very attractive. At least a couple of companies in this list come with an elevated level of risk. We urge due diligence to determine if it would suit your personal situation. Nothing comes for free, so there will be more risk involved with this group. That said, it's a highly diversified group spread among five different industry segments.

We would like to caution that each company comes with certain risks and concerns. Sometimes, these risks are real, but other times, they may be a bit overblown and temporary. So, it's always recommended to do further research and due diligence.

What If We Were to Combine the Three Lists?

If we combine the three lists and remove the duplicates (because of combining), we would be left with ten names.

Two-time duplicates: MS, MPLX, RIO, V(4 duplicates)

Three-time duplicates: LYB (2 duplicates).

After removing these SIX duplicates, we are left with nine names.

The combined list is highly diversified in as many as eight different industry segments. The stats for the group of 9 are as follows:

Average yield: 5.77%

Average discount (from 52WK High): -23.2%

Average 5-yr dividend growth: 8.7%

Average 10-yr dividend growth: 13.3% (from 7 names)

Average Payout Ratio: 46.1%

Average Total Quality Score: 72.5

Table 2:

Conclusion:

In this article, we have presented three groups of dividend-paying stocks (five each) with different goals in mind to suit the varying needs of a wider audience. Even though the risk profile of each group is different, each group in itself is fairly balanced and diversified.

This month, the first group yields 4.2%, while the second group elevates the yield to 5.7%. We also presented a C-list for yield-hungry investors with an 8.1% yield. The combined group (all three lists combined with duplication removed) offers an even more diversified group with nine positions and a 5.77% yield.

data.title: Your Retirement Starts Here: 2 Dividend Gems I'd Trust With My Future
data.description:
People invest in stocks for two opposite reasons - in hope and confidence in the future of an enterprise or in fear that the value of their capital will be lost through inflation. - Bernhard Baruch

Introduction

Earlier this week, I read an article that shocked me.

The article, which was published by the Dutch public news broadcaster NOS, discussed the latest inflation numbers in my country.

After a 4.1% inflation rate in April, inflation fell to 3.3%. This is semi-good news, as it is still 130 basis points above the European Central Bank's 2.0% target, and yet another above-average number since the 2021 inflation surge, as we can clearly see in the chart below.

Technically speaking, Dutch inflation numbers are not important for the world. It's a nation with roughly 17 million people that does not tend to influence global markets and geopolitics.

However, I still had to bring it up, as the same developments are seen all over the world, as inflation is settling in the 3-4% range, after years of high-mid-digit inflation, which has created a massive cumulative price spike since 2021.

Even worse, when looking closer, we see some very ugly developments, including the cost of essential items like food and beverages. The prices of Dutch daily groceries were 7% higher, similar to the price increase in April.

These are scary numbers, as grocery shopping has become incredibly expensive, causing many to opt for the cheapest products or use food stamps/special handouts to fill gaps in their weekly budgets.

In the United States, the same is visible, as CNBC reported last month:

Amid broader inflation, 77% of Americans say they have felt the most significant increase in grocery costs, according to a March report by Credit Karma. No wonder: Food prices have risen 24% since 2020, according to a February report from the Economic Research Service.

[...] From February 2022 to August 2024, as food prices swelled, credit card delinquencies increased 39.8%, according to a recent report by the Urban Institute. In 2023, the research firm found that about a quarter of adults paid for groceries on a credit card and took on debt as a result. - CNBC

Food prices have risen roughly 30% since January 1, 2020.

Obviously, there are many ways to lower the pain of high food inflation, including better meal planning, stacking discounts, buying store brands, making specific purchases at certain stores, and others.

In this case, I'm just using a few measures that CNBC listed in its article.

The point, however, isn't that we need to cut costs but that we are, once again, faced by the ugly reality of inflation, as people are literally going into debt to buy basic goods.

No surprise that everyone's depressed!

While fear and anxiety are obviously caused by a variety of factors, the uptrend since 2020 is something that worries me a lot, and I expect inflation and general economic fears to play a major role.

The Wall Street Journal discussed this as well, in an article titled "Should Two Retirees Panic About Inflation? He's Worried. She Says Relax."

Steve is one of the worriers. I think we all know a 'Steve'.

“I’m really starting to worry about inflation,” Steve told me as we biked to the beach. “It’s getting scary.”

I’d heard it all before. Steve has worried about running short of money since we began dating in 1977, even when our bank account got to the point where it was healthy and our expenditures were stable. Inflation is just the latest trigger. - WSJ

While Steve in this example may be a consistent worrier, the article certainly brings up a few good points that a lot of people are currently dealing with, including the impact of inflation on the many things we need to take care of in our daily lives:

Well, no, Steve replied, at least not soon, but our money won’t go as far, and we may need to start dipping into our IRAs sooner than we’d hoped, and the house needs repairs that will certainly cost more than expected—did you hear what the electrician said about the price of copper wire?—and travel costs are up so we may need to rethink those plans, and maybe we can’t give as much to charity, and restaurant prices are crazy, how can we enjoy eating out? - WSJ (emphasis added)

Good thing Steve has a financial planner he trusts, as he provided him with a few good ideas:

  • Stick to a prudent investment strategy.
  • Do not change your lifestyle too much.
  • Divide your retirement into three buckets.

Here are the three buckets:

  1. The bond ladder portfolio consists of short-term and medium-term bonds designed to provide monthly income until Social Security kicks in and Steve and his wife start tapping into their IRAs.
  2. This bucket consists of all-weather stocks and bonds, with moderate growth and inflation protection.
  3. Bucket No. 3 consists of medium-risk to medium-high-risk investments for later years. This includes a mix of traditional and Roth IRAs. Essentially, capital is expected to flow from bucket 3 to bucket 1 (through bucket 2) over time.

The challenge is that for everyone reading this, the situation varies. Some are 20-year-old college students eager to start investing, while others are 80 years old and need to make the most of what they have. In the middle are people like my parents, who are 5 to 7 years away from retirement and are gradually shifting to income-generating stocks.

Hence, in the second part of this article, I decided to focus on two stocks that work for everyone, with the emphasis on a few key issues:

  • They have great yields, which are protected by consistently elevated dividend growth against inflation.
  • They have fantastic business models that will allow these companies to survive even severe recessions.
  • Both stocks are attractively priced, with a high likelihood of strong long-term total returns, which allows even younger investors to buy some income without sacrificing long-term capital returns.

In other words, while I went with a super catchy title again, I still stick to two of my core principles, which are that I will never lie to you and never clickbait you.

I truly believe these are two of the best income stocks a retiree can own.

MPLX LP (MPLX) - A Near Unbeatable Mix Of High Income And Growth

MPLX is a Master Limited Partnership. It issues a K-1 form. This also explains why shares are called 'units' and dividends are called 'distributions'.

Every time I discuss MPLX, I mention that I would have bought it many years ago if buying MLPs weren't such a headache for non-American investors.

That's why I always cover this midstream giant with bittersweet feelings, as I consider this company a superior high-yield stock. But that's OK because readers have been doing well holding the company, as we can see below.

One of the reasons why I love this company so much is its strong relationship with Marathon Petroleum (MPC), one of America's largest refiners and its largest shareholder, as MPLX is a spin-off from MPC.

This relationship is key, as it improves MPLX's ability to optimize crude and product logistics solutions, supporting MPC's refining operations across the Gulf Coast, Mid-Continent, and West Coast regions, as the map below shows.

Essentially, it provides both companies with reliable partners, which gives MPLX a lot more safety when planning new investments, while MPC uses the distributions it receives for its own capital spending plans.

This safety also allowed MPLX to hike its distribution in 2020, when lockdowns caused a total implosion in demand for refined products and related energy.

On top of that, I love that MPLX is engaging in major projects to expand its footprint in America's massive energy industry. In fact, when it comes to taking on major projects, MPLX is one of my favorites in the industry.

In 1Q25, the company engaged in more than $1 billion worth of M&A:

  • BANGL NGL Pipeline System: MPLX is buying the remaining 55% interest in the BANGL pipeline. This acquisition improves its Permian footprint by connecting NGL production to Gulf Coast fractionation facilities. Moreover, this mission-critical pipeline completed a capacity expansion to 250,000 barrels per day (bpd) in the first quarter and is seeing progress toward a further expansion to 300,000 bpd by the second half of next year.
  • Whiptail Midstream Gathering Business: This project was acquired in March for $237 million and includes crude oil, natural gas, and water gathering assets in the San Juan Basin. Essentially, it improves the company's aforementioned strategic relationship with MPC by supporting connectivity to its El Paso refinery.
  • Matterhorn Express Pipeline: MPLX doubled its stake from 5% to 10%, with the transaction expected to close in the current quarter. This pipeline improves the company's natural gas transportation network and provides much-needed egress for a region in the Permian where a natural gas glut has pressured pricing. As an investor in Permian landowners, the Matterhorn is key for me, as it increases overall pricing power.

For the full-year it also plans to invest $1.7 billion worth of growth capital, with 85% of this capital glowing into natural gas and natural gas liquids services, which is - by far - my favorite midstream segment due to the favorable demand and supply dynamics, supported by data centers, LNG exports, electrification, and more.

This includes multi-billion-dollar long-term investments in Gulf Coast fractionation facilities and the Harmon Creek III Processing Plant in the mighty Marcellus Basin, which brings the company's Northeast gas processing capacity to 8.1 billion cubic feet per day, making it a cornerstone of the entire region's future.

All of this means that the company gets to grow along with the booming natural gas industry without (direct) pricing risks, as its contracts are all fee-based, with 75% of all deals based on take-or-pay protection, which means customers pay for pipeline space, regardless of whether they use it or not.

Thanks to these operations, it has grown its EBITDA by 6.8% per year since 2021. Its distributable cash flow has grown by a similar rate, allowing the company to grow its distribution (dividend) by 10.7% per year.

This is a fantastic distribution growth rate in light of a current yield of 7.4%.

Furthermore, the distribution is protected by a 3.3x net leverage ratio and a coverage ratio of 1.5x, indicating a lot of room for future dividend growth.

Given its yield and growth potential, MPLX remains a Strong Buy in my book.

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) - One Of My All-Time Favorite Dividend Growers

Ironically, I don't own it.

However, it has been on my watchlist for years, as I have been a fan of this company since the day I started researching it.

Now, I'm looking to buy it for my own portfolio and my parents', as I truly believe it offers a nearly unbeatable mix of inflation-protected income, growth, and extreme safety - all covered under a thick layer of secular growth.

I also believe ELS is one of the most underappreciated real estate investment trusts ("REITs") on the market, which may be due to its 3.3% yield. Investors looking for income often opt for a company like Realty Income (O), which currently has a yield that hovers between 5.5% and 6.0%.

As much as I like Realty Income, ELS offers more than just a yield.

Equity Lifestyle, which owns 455 properties primarily consisting of manufactured housing communities, has delivered a 14% return since its IPO in 1993. Over the past ten years, it has achieved more than 200%, significantly outperforming the Dow Jones Equity ALL REIT Index (+81%).

In other words, while its yield may be slightly lower, total wealth creation has been superior, supported by normalized/adjusted per-share funds from operations ("AFFO") growth of 8.4% between 2006 and 2024, above the residential REIT and total REIT industry averages of 5.0% and 3.8%, respectively.

During this period, its dividend grew by 11.4%, a growth rate roughly twice as high as its residential REIT peers. On a longer-term basis, this means ELS investors end up with a higher yield on cost and a better total return than most higher-yielding REITs. Its most recent dividend hike was 7.9% on February 6, which is more than three times the sector median's 10-year CAGR.

I'm sure of long-term outperformance because the company has a superior business model. Its focus on high-quality manufactured housing communities makes it a major beneficiary of significant affordability issues in the housing market.

According to the company, it maintains a 94% occupancy rate in its MH communities, with 97% of all sites being occupied by homeowners. This means that people own their assets and pay monthly lot fees, which are often tied to CPI inflation and enable ELS to maintain its communities, including the many amenities such as pools, tennis courts, and much more.

According to the company, homeowners usually remain in its communities for an average of 10 years, which substantially lowers revenue volatility. Moreover, home prices in ELS communities range from $80,000 to $100,000, which is significantly lower than single-family home prices in key markets like Florida ($370,000 to $460,000), California ($1 million to $1.4 million), and Arizona ($425,000).

While MH communities cannot always compete with single-family homes, the quality has become much better over the past two decades, which makes it a fantastic market for retirees, who enjoy mostly anti-cyclical income.

Also, as we can see below, the total monthly costs of MH community homes are 66% below the single-family home average, with the biggest difference being higher upfront costs. However, even MH assets can be financed nowadays.

The same goes for its RV sites, which benefit from more than 75% annual recurring revenue, with many customers paying up to $6,000 in northern markets and $8,000 in Sunbelt markets to have access to its lots.

In other words, this business model comes with strong secular growth from outperforming population in key markets, a rapidly aging population benefiting age-restricted communities, inflation-protected (and mostly anti-cyclical) rent income, and a business that is easy to maintain and scale.

It also has a fantastic balance sheet, which comes with a weighted average debt maturity of 8.4 years and only $87 million in debt maturing before 2028 (9% of total debt, compared to the REIT average of 30%).

Meanwhile, the company's debt-to-EBITDA ratio is 4.4x, and interest coverage is 5.4x. This comes with $1 billion in liquidity, including its credit lines.

Looking ahead, analysts expect 5% per-share AFFO growth in both 2025 and 2026, potentially followed by an acceleration to 7% after that. When adding its dividend, we get a total return outlook of at least 10% on a longer-term basis, which I expect to get a boost from lower long-term rates in the years ahead.

If rates come down, we'll likely see accelerating flows into high-quality dividend stocks and stronger housing demand. This will more than likely fuel home prices, giving ELS an even bigger relative advantage.

Unless investors are dependent on every investment paying at least 5% or 6% in annual dividends (or any high number), I believe it is very hard to find a better long-term investment.

Takeaway

Inflation isn't just a number. It's a daily struggle, especially when essentials like groceries cost 30% more than in 2020.

While Dutch inflation might seem irrelevant globally, the pain is universal, as people are going into debt just to eat. When adding other challenges, it is no surprise that people like Steve in my example get anxious.

Luckily, there are ways to fight back, which is what this article was all about.

MPLX, which comes with a 7.4% yield, and Equity LifeStyle Properties, with a 3.3% yield, stand out as resilient income generators, backed by fantastic business models.

MPLX benefits from its mission-critical energy infrastructure, with impressive growth projects and a rock-solid high yield. Meanwhile, ELS leverages housing affordability issues, as it offers inflation-protected rents and long-term upside with strong secular growth.

While none of these stocks offer a quick fix, I believe they are the perfect tools to build resilient portfolios that allow us to beat inflation, grow income, and create substantial long-term wealth.

Risks To My Thesis

A steep economic downturn could pressure energy demand and hurt ELS's cyclical income from RV fees. Moreover, sticky inflation could also keep rates elevated, pressuring valuations.

On top of that, both stocks are prone to general market risks.

The good news is that MPLX and ELS are very resilient, which means even if we were to enter a very steep recession, I do not expect dividend cuts or any major damage to their financials.

data.title: Opportunities In The Lone Star State: Inside The Russell Texas Equity Index
data.description:
By Catherine Yoshimoto, Director, Product Management, Equity Index Products

In response to growing investor demand, FTSE Russell recently introduced the Russell Texas Equity Index as part of the Russell US State Equity Index Series. This new index is focused on companies based in the economic engine of Texas and is derived from FTSE Russell’s flagship US equity index series, the Russell US Indexes. The Russell US Indexes have approximately $10.6 trillion in associated assets benchmarked.[1]

Let's explore what's fueling investor interest in Texan companies and how the index is constructed.

Accessing a dynamic and diversified economy

Investors are increasingly seeking tailored exposure to the distinct economic profiles and growth potential of individual US states. Texas stands out as a particularly compelling case. By itself, Texas represents the eighth-largest economy in the world, and it is home to one in ten publicly traded US companies[2]. Looking ahead, Texas ranks first among US states in five-year growth projections—an outlook driven by a business-friendly regulatory environment, absence of state income tax, robust infrastructure and a strategic geographic location.

While historically known for oil and gas, the Texas economy has diversified significantly into technology, healthcare, financial services and manufacturing. This evolution has been supported by a wave of corporate relocations, with major companies like Tesla, Charles Schwab, and Oracle moving their headquarters to the state.

The Russell Texas Equity Index offers investors a straightforward way to capture the momentum of this transformation, providing access to a thriving and evolving economy with strong prospects for continued growth.

Index construction

The index methodology is simple and designed with representativeness and investability in mind.

Starting with the Russell 3000® Index, eligible constituents are identified based on the following criteria:

  • Inclusion is limited to companies that are headquartered in Texas.
  • Securities with an investable market capitalisation below USD $500 million are excluded.
  • Securities with a three-month rolling average daily trading volume lower than USD $3 million are excluded.

The index is initially weighted based on the float-adjusted market capitalisation of each index constituent. Capping is then applied following the RIC 10/45 methodology at the quarterly index reviews.

This ensures that, at each review, no more than 10% of the index’s weight is allocated to any individual company, and the sum of the weights of all constituents representing more than 4.5% of the index does not exceed 45% of the index in aggregate. Between quarterly reviews, index weights are allowed to drift from these thresholds to reflect changes in the market.

The Russell US State Equity Index Series is reviewed quarterly at the stock market close of the third Friday in March, September, and December. June’s re-weighting is completed at the same time as the annual reconstitution of the Russell US Indexes.

As shown below, the Russell Texas Equity Index offers diversified industry exposure and, based on a back-test, it outperformed the Russell 3000 index over one-, three-, and five-year periods ending March 31, 2025.

In summary, Texas continues to distinguish itself with strong growth and sector diversity (see the pie chart for the Russell Texas Equity Index ICB Industry breakdown).

For equity investors seeking to complement broad US exposure, the Russell Texas Equity Index provides a rules-based tool to implement single-state investment in this dynamic economy.

data.title: International Workplace Group: Share Price Rally Is Not Supported By Earnings Growth
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, while I was not particularly bullish on International Workplace Group plc (IWGFF) as I’m an income-oriented investor and the company’s dividend yield is not great, its share price has performed better than I was expecting over the past few months. Indeed, since my previous article on IWG, its total return is about 30%, beating the overall stock market by a great margin during the same period.

This positive performance has been supported by the company’s operating momentum and growth strategy, with IWG gradually shifting its business toward capital-light activities, and also by its more aggressive capital returns than expected. Since my previous analysis on IWG, the company has reported its 2024 results, which can be considered positive as the company was able to achieve earnings and cash flow growth despite reporting a stable top-line.

Indeed, IWG’s business mix is slowly shifting away from its traditional company-owned space into managed & franchised, even though company-owned is still that largest segment by far. In 2024, its total revenues amounted to $3.69 billion, being practically flat compared to the previous year, as the company-owned segment and digital & professional services reported a slight decline in revenues that was offset by growth in the managed & franchised unit.

As can be seen in the previous table, its growth engine is clearly the managed & franchised segment, with system revenue being up by 17% YoY to $620 million, leading to a 29% increase in annual revenue to $79 million in 2024. This means IWG’s contribution margin in this segment was about 13% over the last year, representing an improvement of more than 100 basis points (bps) compared to the previous year. This shows that its business is maturing in this segment, being a good sign for higher margins ahead.

Investors should note that IWG’s growth strategy changed toward its managed & franchised segment a few years ago, as the company wants to have a business model that is not based on owning properties but re-letting space instead for flexible office solutions and collecting fees from these services. While the vast majority of its revenues still come from the company-owned segment, the most earnings and cash flow growth come from the managed & franchised segment, being a trend that is likely to persist over the coming years.

This growth has been supported by its strategy of opening more locations, with about 62,000 net room openings during 2024, increasing the total number of rooms in operation to about 185,000 at the end of last year. Its pipeline of future rooms to open amounted to around 182,000, showing that its growth potential in this segment is strong.

According to IWG, new openings take about 18 months to reach revenue maturity, which means fee income is expected to maintain a growing trajectory over the coming years, as recent openings take some time to reach maturity and new openings should be an important tailwind for system-wide revenue growth and increasing fee income for IWG.

Supported by a higher number of rooms in operation, IWG’s fee income increased by 30% YoY to $79 million in 2024. This only represented about 2.2% of its total revenues in the year, but investors should take into consideration that IWG’s operating leverage in this segment is high, as costs associated with these operations are low and capital expenditures are also minimal. This means higher fee income directly contributes to margin expansion and higher free cash flow, even though the size of this segment is still quite low.

The company expects the annual system-wide revenue to be around $1.4 billion when all rooms currently in the pipeline are open, which could lead to annual fee income for IWG of about $180 million assuming stable contribution margins. This means fee income from the managed & franchised segment can more than double over the next few years, being an important support for earnings and free cash flow growth ahead.

As the company continues to exit locations in the company-owned segment and doesn’t want to grow much this unit, its maintenance and growth capex has been declining in recent years and this trend is expected to be maintained over the coming years. In 2024, maintenance capex declined by 9% YoY to $93 million and growth capex was $57 million (-27% YoY), being an important driver of higher free cash flow in the segment.

Overall, IWG’s cash flow from business operations amounted to $205 million in 2024, compared to $165 million in the previous year. After interest and taxes, its free cash flow was $97 million, which the company used to reduce its indebtedness ($63 million) and to distribute dividends to shareholders ($17 million).

The combination of a higher EBITDA ($557 million in 2024, representing annual growth of 10.7%) and a lower net debt level ($712 million at the end of 2024 vs. $775 million in 2023), led to a leverage ratio of 1.3x (vs. 1.5x in 2023). This shows that IWG is on the right track to reach its desired leverage ratio of about 1x over the coming months, being a relatively low leverage ratio within the real estate industry.

This allows the company to provide a more attractive shareholder remuneration policy, both through dividends and share buybacks. Following several years of dividend suspension, IWG resumed dividend payments related to 2023 earnings, at £0.01 per share, a level that was maintained related to 2024 earnings. At its current share price, this leads to a dividend yield of about 0.66%, which is quite low and shows that IWG’s income appeal is minimal. On top of its dividend, IWG also announced a $50 million share buyback program some months ago, representing around 1.75% of its current market value. While capital returns are still relatively low, the company is giving shareholders a sign that it wants to increasingly return capital to shareholders in the future, possibly being more sizable to shareholders when the company reaches its desired leverage ratio.

Assuming IWG will continue to grow its annual EBITDA at around 10% and reduce debt levels by around $60 million per year, I think IWG’s leverage ratio can be close to 1.05x at the end of 2025. Therefore, IWG could be in a position that allows it to distribute most of its free cash flow generation to shareholders related to 2026 earnings, both through dividends and share buybacks.

Indeed, according to analysts’ estimates, its dividend is expected to grow considerably over the next few years, to about £0.036 per share by 2027. However, as the company’s dividend would still be relatively low, its dividend yield is not expected to be above 2% over the coming years, showing that IWG’s income appeal should not be great in the near term.

Regarding its operating performance in the first quarter of 2025, IWG only reports a trading update and does not disclose full financial figures but has maintained a positive operating momentum regarding its disclosed metrics. Its system-wide revenue increased by 2% YoY to $1.057 billion, with the company continuing to open new rooms in managed & franchise and signing new locations. However, group revenue was flat at $909 million compared to Q1 2024, as strong fee revenue growth in managed & franchised (+43% YoY to $23 million) was offset by a decline in digital & professional services of 13% YoY to $84 million.

Due to positive cash flow generation, its net debt declined by $4 million in the quarter to $708 million and IWG maintained its EBITDA guidance for the year to be between $580-620 million, being on the right track to achieve a leverage ratio closer to 1x by the end of 2025.

Regarding share buybacks, at the beginning of last May, IWG had already completed more than half of its $50 million share buyback program and announced another $50 million to be completed over the next few months, showing the company’s commitment to enhance capital returns to shareholders. While it’s not certain, IWG may further increase the share buyback program after the summer when it announces its first semester results, as the company is clearly switching its strategy from balance sheet deleveraging toward higher capital returns.

Regarding its valuation, due to an increasing share price in recent months that was above the company’s EBITDA growth, IWG’s current valuation is now more expensive than it was when I last covered it. IWG is currently trading at around 6x EV/EBITDA based on its expected EBITDA of $600 million in 2025 (middle of its guidance range), while previously was trading at 5.3x EBITDA. This shows, in my opinion, that IWG’s strong share price performance lately has been driven by a more aggressive capital return strategy rather than earnings growth, which can be concerning for investors regarding upside potential in the near term.

Conclusion

International Workplace Group’s operating momentum in recent quarters has been positive, as the managed & franchised segment continues to grow its size and leads to higher business margins and cash flows. This has allowed the company to reduce debt and offer a more attractive shareholder remuneration policy, something that has been rewarded by investors in recent months. However, its income appeal remains quite low and this is not expected to change over the coming years, plus its valuation seems to be fair, and therefore I maintain my ‘hold’ recommendation for the time being.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The More It Drops, The More I Buy
data.description:
High yield stocks whose prices have taken a tumble recently are often very attractive to investors. This is because the high yield combined with the allure of an apparent discount makes many investors think that they are able to lock in a high income yield along with significant capital appreciation potential as the stock price recovers over time, without the business needing to generate any underlying intrinsic value growth.

However, just because a stock offers a high yield and has pulled back recently is far from a guarantor of success. In fact, it should serve as a yellow flag that prompts you to take a closer look to see why the stock has pulled back as much as it has. Typically, it is due to growing market concerns over the health of the balance sheet—or even worse—a brewing major disruption in its industry that could end up leading to massive losses of shareholder capital. Some past examples of this include NextEra Energy Partners (XIFR), when its balance sheet became significantly over-leveraged in the face of skyrocketing interest rates, or Class B and C mall REITs when the Amazon disruption began to rear its ugly head. In both cases, investors who piled in to buy the dips got burned badly.

However, there are other times when a high-yield stock’s price dips that it truly presents a compelling buying opportunity. This is most often the case when the business itself and the balance sheet are on sound footing, and the stock price simply pulled back due to a short-term headwind for the business—or in some cases, not even a headwind for the business, but simply negative headlines—yet the long-term trajectory of the business and its industry remain on sound footing. In this article, I will share two such stocks that I have recently invested in on the dip and plan to buy more of if they dip significantly further.

Buy The Dip Opportunity #1

The first stock I will discuss is Easterly Government Properties (DEA). The stock has taken a huge tumble in recent years due to three major headwinds.

The first one is that as a triple net lease REIT like Realty Income (O), it has suffered immensely from the sharp rise in interest rates over the past three years, as its business model is often considered a bond proxy. Therefore, when long-term interest rates rise, investors expect a higher yield from the stock to compensate them for the equity risk they take on relative to treasuries. Additionally, with rising interest rates, the company's cost of capital also increases, which makes it harder for it to grow at the same rate that it would have been able to otherwise had it had a lower cost of capital.

The second major headwind that it has faced is negative headlines surrounding the Trump administration's Department of Government Efficiency ("DOGE") initiative. This is because DEA invests in properties that it leases to the federal government, as well as some state governments and other government agencies. As such, there was a fear that there would be mass layoffs of government employees and large segments of departments, if not entire departments and agencies, being significantly degraded or even altogether eliminated. Therefore, there was a concern that there would be numerous leases canceled or at the very least not renewed or even forcibly renegotiated.

However, this is simply not the case, as DEA has lengthy lease terms, it invests in mission-critical properties and agencies that are highly unlikely to be disrupted by the Trump administration, and the vast majority of its rent comes from leases that cannot be unilaterally canceled by the federal government (for more on all of this, check out DEA's latest investor presentation here). In fact, management has reported that they see DOGE efforts as actually being a tailwind to the company because there is a lot of deferred maintenance in government properties, and in many cases they are even working alongside DOGE to help them identify properties where the government can more efficiently handle its resources by selling assets that they own instead of leasing them from companies like DEA. Moreover, with Elon Musk recently formally leaving the Trump administration and having a huge falling out with President Trump, it is less likely than ever that DOGE is actually going to have the scope of impact needed to truly impact DEA’s business.

The third and final headwind that has driven DEA’s sharp decline in stock price is that they recently slashed their dividend. Note that they did not slash this dividend because they were forced to by their balance sheet or even not covering their dividend with cash flow, but instead because they wanted to be able to retain more cash flow to take advantage of growth opportunities given that their cost of equity and debt have become prohibitively high in the current environment. However, that is now in the rearview mirror and DEA’s dividend is now on much more sustainable footing, while its very stable, defensive cash flow profile should make it very sustainable for years to come. This makes the only remaining headwind to take into account the higher-for-longer interest rate environment. While there is certainly a risk that long-term interest rates could push still higher—especially given the sustained deficits being run by the federal government—much of this is already priced in, as DEA currently sits at a huge 33% discount to its analyst consensus net asset value.

With a dividend yield of 9% and expected growth of 3%+ per year based on management's recent guidance issued in its earnings call, DEA appears to offer a very attractive risk-reward profile with potential for double-digit annualized total returns without even accounting for potential valuation multiple upside. Moreover, if interest rates eventually begin to fall, DEA could see a massive re-rating higher, both in terms of its valuation multiples but also due to a potentially lower cost of capital that could enable it to reignite its growth machine.

Buy The Dip Opportunity #2

The second opportunity that I bought on its recent sharp dip is midstream infrastructure business Energy Transfer (ET) (note that it issues a K1 tax form). While its decline was not nearly as sharp as DEA's, it still provides compelling value. This stems from multiple sources. First of all, its dip is largely due to concerns about Trump's trade war, and in particular between the United States and China, given that the U.S. exports a lot of energy commodities to China via companies like ET. Additionally, ET has reported that the Trump administration has been interfering with its ability to export at least some of these commodities to China, while China has also put some constraints on its importation of some of the other commodities that ET exports as part of this trade war.

However, the Trump administration recently reported that Trump and Xi had a good conversation and that there will be talks this coming Monday between the two sides to try to put trade relations back on a good footing. While it is hard to know exactly how these trade agreements will work themselves out, and it is hard to be too bullish on the long-term trend of U.S. and China relations, the good news for a company like ET is that there is broad demand across the globe for its energy product exports. Therefore, even if trade between the U.S. and China became choppy again in the future, there would likely be other markets that the U.S. could export these energy products to, and eventually the entire global markets would balance themselves out. In fact, it appears that importing additional American energy seems to be at the heart of the negotiations between the U.S. and many of its other trade partners, which could help offset and potentially even more than offset any headwinds that ET may suffer from due to trade friction between the U.S. and China. Moreover, ET just signed a 20-year agreement to export commodities to a company in Japan.

Meanwhile, ET's cash flow profile seems highly stable, with about 90% of its expected EBITDA this year coming from contracted cash flows and only about 10% of it being commodity price sensitive. Additionally, with a distribution yield of nearly 8%, a stellar balance sheet with a BBB credit rating, a lot of free cash flow, a distribution coverage ratio of almost two times, and an expected distribution growth profile in the mid-single digits, as well as an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple that is well below that of its peers like MPLX (MPLX) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), ET appears to be a highly compelling buy-the-dip opportunity.

Investor Takeaway

Between DEA and ET, we see two businesses with solid business models, balance sheets, and attractive and sustainable dividends, along with inflation-meeting or beating growth potential, and what appear to be overblown market concerns. As a result, I think both are highly compelling buys right now, and I would gladly buy even more on any future dips in their stock prices. These are the sort of opportunities that we load up on at High Yield Investor.

I have wonderful news for anyone worried about the stock market being in a bubble.

Stock Gains Over 16 Years Have Been Justified By Fundamentals

The good news is that around 91% of the gains seen since 2009 have been justified by fundamentals, including dividends, earnings growth, and net cash on balance sheets.

What You Need To Know About Today's Valuation

S&P Cash-Adjusted PEGY Valuation (PE/Yield + Growth): Scary Overvalued! But There Is A Catch!

S&P 10-Year Median Yield 1.77%
S&P 10-year CAGR FCF/Share Growth 9.60%
S&P 10-Year Median PEGY Ratio 1.16
S&P 17-Year Median Yield 1.88%
S&P 17-Year FCF/Share Growth 8.22%
S&P 17-Year Median PEGY Ratio 1.17
S&P Current Yield 1.33%
S&P LT Growth Consensus: 9.19%
S&P Current PEGY Ratio 1.39
S&P PEGY Overvaluation 19.9%
Upside To Fair Value -16.58%
12-Month Forward EPS Growth 11.2%
12-Month Fundamentally Justified Total Return Potential -4.0%
12-Month Fundamentally Justified S&P 5,696.11

(Source: FactSet, Dividend Kings S&P Valuation Tool)

Morningstar just downgraded its long-term (five-year) EPS growth forecast from 9.67% to 9.19% CAGR.

  • The S&P 500 has experienced a 6.2% compound annual growth rate in its historical earnings per share growth rate since its inception in 1957.

Wait a second? Doesn't history suggest that we can expect potentially strong returns from US stocks within a year?

This is where the importance of understanding investing models comes in.

All Models Are Wrong: Some Are Useful

"All models are wrong, but some are useful." UK Statician George E.P Box

Many investors focus on a specific metric, such as market valuations (like PE ratios) or 12-month price targets.

  • Like Morgan Stanley's recent 6,500 end-of-year target (approximately 10% upside from here).

Here's what's critical to remember about valuation models and stocks.

Time Frame (Years) Total Returns Explained By Fundamentals/Valuations
1 Day 0.02%
1 month 0.39%
3 month 1.2%
6 months 3.5%
1 7% (12 Month Price Forecasts)
2 14%
3 21%
4 28%
5 29%
6 54%
7 63%
8 72%
9 81%
10+ 90%
20+ 91%
30+ 97%

(Source: JPMorgan, Bank of America, Fidelity, RIA, Princeton)

Morgan Stanley estimates a $ 6,500 year-end estimate by estimating EPS for the entire market (from all its analysts) and then applying a P/E.

  • 20.1X forward PE in this case.

How does Morgan Stanley come to that estimate? It's an educated guesstimate (usually created by running Monte Carlo simulations).

However, remember that, according to JPMorgan, only 7% of stock returns over 12 months are explained by fundamentals (such as EPS), while 93% are attributed to sentiment, momentum, and luck (as reflected in the PE ratio).

The Wrong and Right Way To Think About Short-Term Market Forecasts and Valuations

Let me show you what happens if you change long-term growth expectations (five-year growth estimates).

11.7% CAGR 5-Year EPS Growth Does This to Market Valuations

S&P 10-Year Median Yield 1.77%
S&P 10-year CAGR FCF/Share Growth 9.60%
S&P 10-Year Median PEGY Ratio 1.16
S&P 17-Year Median Yield 1.88%
S&P 17-Year FCF/Share Growth 8.22%
S&P 17-Year Median PEGY Ratio 1.17
S&P Current Yield 1.33%
S&P LT Growth Consensus: 11.70%
S&P Current PEGY Ratio 1.12
S&P PEGY Overvaluation -3.2%
Upside To Fair Value 3.32%
12-Month Forward EPS Growth 11.2%
12-Month Fundamentally Justified Total Return Potential 15.9%
12-Month Fundamentally Justified S&P 6,882.72

(Source: FactSet, Dividend Kings S&P Valuation Tool)

If the S&P's five-year EPS growth rate is 11.7%, then stocks aren't 20% overvalued, but 3% undervalued.

  • Morningstar's S&P bottom-up weighted valuation model says stocks are 2% undervalued.

Why? Morningstar's S&P valuation model is called a bottom-up model.

  • Each analyst creates a discounted cash flow model to estimate the fair value for every company in the S&P.
  • And then market cap-weight those fair value estimates, and you get a 2% discount.

A discounted cash flow model requires estimating not just growth for a company in the future, but also a discount rate that you think investors will demand for each company.

  • Think of it like each analyst guessing what the PE ratio will be for each company they cover.

I let the market's very own PEGY ratio tell me what the market is likely to value itself at.

  • Enterprise value/EBITDA replaces PE (think "cash adjusted PE")
  • Yield + Growth replaces growth in the PEG ratio.

Peter Lynch popularized the PEG ratio because "growth at a reasonable price" helped him buy companies that were trading at high PEs but were worth it, justified by their growth rates.

I've added EV/EBITDA (the most popular metric among private equity firms) because it calculates the cost of actually buying a company (including cash and debt) and yield plus growth (total returns over the long term).

It's a model that explains 91% of the market's returns over the last 16 years (and likely much further).

Why I'm 91% Confident The Market Isn't In An "Everything Bubble"

Many famous investors like Howard Marks or Jeremy Grantham consider stocks overvalued, with Grantham (who famously called the Japanese real estate/stock bubble, tech bubble, and housing bubble) having been calling the US market in a bubble for 15 years.

Here are the longest bubbles in history; the longest one lasted 13 years.

The statistical probability that bubbles can last 16 years is approximately 9%, and those lasting 30 years or more are 3%.

  • Some permabears, like John Hussman, have called the market since 1980 a mega-bubble created by falling interest rates.

OK, so the probability that GMO is right, and we are in for a "lost decade" is not as great as some fear (about 1 in 11).

But what does this have to do with market forecasts like Morgan Stanley's 6,500 market target?

The Right Way To Think About Valuation: What Expectations Are Price In?

Remember how if the S&P's EPS growth is 11.7%, the market is 3% undervalued, and the 12-month S&P fundamentally justified return potential is 6,800?

  • By the end of the year, Morgan Stanley forecasts approximately 6,500, which would be the total.

Why did I pick 11.7% CAGR growth?

S&P FactSet Consensus Growth Through 2027: 11.7% CAGR

Because that's the exact current consensus through 2027, a three-year EPS growth rate of 11.7%.

  • The 20-year accuracy of this bottom-up consensus of all 3,500 analysts on Wall Street is 92%.

Does this mean that Morgan Stanley expects five-year EPS growth to be exactly 11.7%? That today's expected growth rate will continue for two more years, justifying today's historically rich multiple?

Nope. What it does mean is that if and only if the currently expected growth rate is accurate, and it lasts for five years, then based on market returns since 2009, the market is not overvalued, but about the fair value that Morningstar estimates (at the entire company level).

Morningstar's EPS growth estimate fell from 9.7% to 9.2% in recent days. Yet, it estimates that stocks are basically fairly valued.

That's because the collective analyst consensus is saying that in the future, investors will be willing to pay higher PEs (and lower discount rates) for future cash flows and earnings.

No One Is Forecasting Anything: It's All Estimating Growth Probabilities

Wall Street banks are famous not only for having virtually no accuracy at the start of each year, but they also change frequently.

That's because market sentiment drives the "educated guess" that Wall Street banks apply to their consensus EPS estimates, based on the PE ratio.

That's why I never do forecasts, I always talk about consensus fundamentally justified return potential.

  • If and only if earnings grow as analysts expect today, and returns to historical market-determined fair value (the objective discount rate investors have actually given a stock's price over 10+ years), then you'll earn X% total returns over a specific time period.

Think of it like this.

If stock X has 100% total return potential over the next 12 months, it's not a forecast "that stock X will go up 100% by June 2026."

It means that, based on today's expectations, if stock X goes up 100% in the next year, it would be 100% justified by today's best estimate of future fundamentals.

Whether or not it happens is not what I'm trying to determine, only what would be justified by historical market multiples and consensus estimates.

For any individual company, it's rare for a stock to return to fair value within a year. Over the long term? It's a lot easier to estimate blue-chip returns because, in the long term, fundamentals are 33X as powerful as luck.

If you own 10 stocks? Some will rise and some will fall in any given year. Some will go up that should be falling, and vice versa. But overall, over several years, if the businesses grow approximately as expected (at the beginning), you can be highly accurate in terms of achieving the returns you expected.

Dividend Aristocrat Bargains Are All Around Us, Even In "A 20% Overvalued Market"

I recently wrote an article highlighting my favorite blue-chip opportunities, which are the 10 stocks I own personally.

Based on the highest 12-month total return potential.

But what about the dividend aristocrats?

What if I did a "Top 5 Aristocrat" list designed to find the best blue-chip quality aristocrats that were the most coiled spring and Buffett-style "fat pitch?"

Wait for a fat pitch and then swing for the fences." Warren Buffett

Sonoco Products Company (SON)

Becton, Dickson & Co (BDX)

Northwest Natural Holding (NWN)

Federal Realty (FRT)

Novo Nordisk (NVO)

Fundamentals Summary

The yield on these five aristocrats is almost 4%, with a 42% historical discount to fair value, which offers exceptional short- and medium-term return potential, as well as 12% long-term return potential based on yield plus long-term growth consensus.

What's incredible is that these high-quality blue-chips, which have an average credit rating of BBB, and a 50-year dividend growth streak (dividend king portfolio), also offer an incredible 77% 12-month return potential, courtesy of an attractive yield today, strong growth of 8%, and that amazing 42% historical discount.

What Could Go Wrong: Facts Are Always Changing

It's essential to remember that facts are constantly evolving.

For example, the No. 1 aristocrat total return potential for the next year is Sonoco Partners.

  • I just updated its fundamentals, and the summary table doesn't show the update (technical difficulties).

In the past year, SON's big debt-funded acquisition (Eviosys), while significantly improving its diversification geographically and across products (it makes packages like cans and shipping containers), has cost it its BBB credit rating from S&P.

Leverage is now well above 3X safe levels, though S&P has confidence that SON will bring leverage down to 3.5X or less by the end of 2026.

  • Management guidance for 2027 is a return to 3X safe leverage, and the analyst consensus agrees.

However, 50% steel and aluminum tariffs, if they persist for the next 3.5 years, could significantly impact management's deleveraging plans and put the 42-year dividend growth streak at risk.

  • Higher interest rates for longer periods are also pressuring the interest coverage ratio.
  • Reducing the safety score to 64% and the overall quality score to 58% (low quality blue-chip quality).

Currently, things appear to be OK, but the new steel and aluminum tariffs are unexpected and not something management had planned for in its guidance. In contrast, strong 11% to 12% CAGR growth (approximately 2 times the historical norm) is expected, along with steady (though muted) dividend growth through 2027. Things could change.

  • The reason SON is trading at 7X forward earnings is.

The 35% payout ratio is low, but if those tariffs (and a slowing economy) risk the investment grade credit rating, then the dividend safety could fall from safe (down from very safe last year) to unsafe, and dividend cuts are something you want to avoid at all costs.

In other words, the facts are constantly changing, and anyone investing in a single company must accept the risks associated with that investment, including the possibility that today's blue-chip company may become tomorrow's failed aristocrat.

Bottom Line: So Many Wonderful Aristocrat Opportunities Are All Around Us

Market valuation is simple in theory, but it always involves nuance.

That's why the margin of safety is essential because if you are buying a "wonderful company at a fair price" and the fundamentals deteriorate slightly, then you might end up buying a fair company at a high price.

But in the case of these aristocrats, they are trading at a 42% historical discount, which creates a massive margin of safety.

In a market that appears 20% overvalued today (conservatively estimated), these aristocrat coiled springs offer exceptional Buffett-like return potential.

Not from some speculative SPAC or newfangled crypto coin, but from some of the most dependable dividend investors in the world.
